en.extract:
The  African spoonbill  (Platalea alba) is a long-legged wading bird of the ibis and spoonbill family Threskiornithidae. The species is widespread across Africa and Madagascar, including Botswana, Kenya, Mozambique, Namibia, South Africa, and Zimbabwe. The species belongs to the genus Platalea, which also includes species such as the Black-faced spoonbill, Eurasian spoonbill, and Roseate spoonbill. 


Biology
The African spoonbill lives in marshy wetlands with some open shallow water and nests in colonies in trees or reedbeds. They usually don't share colonies with storks or herons. They feed by moving its flat bill side to side throughout shallow water, snapping up fish, molluscs, amphibians, crustaceans, and insects when detected. Long legs and thin, pointed toes enable it to walk easily through varying depths of water. Juveniles are born with shorter bills that gradually develop into the species' unique spoon-shaped form as they mature.
The African spoonbill is almost unmistakable through most of its range. The breeding bird is all white except for its red legs and face and long grey spatulate bill. It has no crest, unlike the common spoonbill. Immature birds lack the red face and have a yellow bill. Unlike herons, spoonbills fly with their necks outstretched.


Breeding

The African spoonbill begins breeding in the winter, which lasts until spring. During the breeding season, adult male African Spoonbills develop more plumage and brighter coloration. The spoonbill's nest, generally located in trees above water, is built from sticks and reeds and lined with leaves. Three to five eggs are laid by the female birds, usually during the months of April or May. The eggs are incubated by both parents for up to 29 days, and upon hatching the young birds are cared for by both parents for around 20 to 30 days. The birds are ready to leave the nest soon afterward, and begin flying after another four weeks.
The African spoonbill is one of the species to which the Agreement on the Conservation of African-Eurasian Migratory Waterbirds (AEWA) applies.


References


Further reading
Grzimek, H. C. Bernhard, ed. (1972). Grzimeks Animal Life Encyclopedia of Birds. New York, New York: Van Nostrand Reinhold Company.
Middleton, Alex L. A. and Dr. Christopher M. Perrins, eds. (1985). The Encyclopedia of Birds. New York, New York: Facts on File, Inc.


External links

African Spoonbill - Species text in The Atlas of Southern African Birds.

The ''' African spoonbill ''' ('''''Platalea alba''''') is a long-legged wading bird<ref name="africanspoonbill">[http://www.honoluluzoo.org/african_spoonbill.htm "African Spoonbill."] {{webarchive |url=https://web.archive.org/web/20110707001812/http://www.honoluluzoo.org/african_spoonbill.htm |date=July 7, 2011 }} [http://www.honoluluzoo.org Honoluluzoo.org]. Accessed June 2011.</ref> of the [[ibis]] and [[spoonbill]] family [[Threskiornithidae]]. The species is widespread across [[Africa]] and [[Madagascar]], including Botswana, Kenya, Mozambique, Namibia, South Africa, and Zimbabwe.<ref name="africanspoonbill"/> The species belongs to the genus ''[[Spoonbill|Platalea]],'' which also includes species such as the [[Black-faced spoonbill]], [[Eurasian spoonbill]], and [[Roseate spoonbill]]. 

==Biology==
The African spoonbill lives in marshy wetlands with some open shallow water and nests in colonies in trees or reedbeds. They usually don't share colonies with [[stork]]s or [[heron]]s. They feed by moving its flat bill side to side throughout shallow water, snapping up [[fish]],<ref name="africanspoonbill"/> [[molluscs]],<ref name="africanspoonbill"/> [[amphibians]], [[crustaceans]],<ref name="africanspoonbill"/> and insects<ref name="africanspoonbill"/> when detected.<ref name="africanspoonbill"/> Long legs and thin, pointed toes enable it to walk easily through varying depths of water.<ref name ="LPZAfricanSpoonbill">{{Cite web |url=http://www.lpzoo.org/animals/factsheet/african-spoonbill |title="African Spoonbill Fact Sheet, Lincoln Park Zoo" |access-date=2011-07-08 |archive-date=2015-12-20 |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20151220190408/http://www.lpzoo.org/animals/factsheet/african-spoonbill }}</ref> Juveniles are born with shorter bills that gradually develop into the species' unique spoon-shaped form as they mature.<ref>{{Cite web |title=African Spoonbill {{!}} Jacksonville Zoo and Gardens |url=https://www.jacksonvillezoo.org/african-spoonbill |access-date=2026-05-07 |website=www.jacksonvillezoo.org}}</ref>

The African spoonbill is almost unmistakable through most of its [[range (biology)|range]]. The breeding bird is all white except for its red legs and face and long grey spatulate [[beak|bill]]. It has no crest, unlike the [[common spoonbill]]. Immature birds lack the red face and have a yellow bill. Unlike herons, spoonbills fly with their necks outstretched.

==Breeding==
[[File:African Spoonbill-001.jpg|thumb|left|African spoonbill in flight]]
[[File:Platalea alba MHNT.ZOO.2010.11.61.5.jpg|thumb|left| ''Platalea alba'' - [[MHNT]]]]
The African spoonbill begins breeding in the winter, which lasts until spring.<ref name="africanspoonbill"/> During the breeding season, adult male African Spoonbills develop more plumage and brighter coloration.<ref>{{cite journal |last1=Kahl |first1=M. P. |title=Displays of the African Spoonbill Platalea Alba |journal=Ibis |date=July 2008 |volume=125 |pages=324–338|doi=10.1111/j.1474-919X.1983.tb03118.x }}</ref> The spoonbill's nest, generally located in trees above water, is built from sticks and reeds and lined with leaves.<ref name ="LPZAfricanSpoonbill" /> Three to five eggs are laid by the female birds, usually during the months of April or May.<ref name="africanspoonbill"/> The eggs are incubated by both parents for up to 29 days, and upon hatching the young birds are cared for by both parents for around 20 to 30 days.<ref name="africanspoonbill"/> The birds are ready to leave the nest soon afterward, and begin flying after another four weeks.<ref name="africanspoonbill"/>

The African spoonbill is one of the species to which the ''Agreement on the Conservation of African-Eurasian Migratory Waterbirds'' ([[AEWA]]) applies.

zh.extract:
非洲琵鷺（學名：Platalea alba）是䴉科琵鹭属一種琵鷺。其种加词“alba”意为“白色的”。牠們廣泛分佈在非洲及馬達加斯加。
非洲琵鷺棲息在沼澤濕地，並會在樹上或蘆葦床墊築巢。牠們一般都不會與鸛科或鷺科分享棲息地。牠們一般都會生2-4隻蛋。
非洲琵鷺很易辨認。繁殖期是全身白色，腳及面部都是紅色的，喙長而呈灰色，沒有冠。未成熟的幼鳥面部不是紅色的，喙呈黃色。牠們飛行時頸部向外伸出。
非洲琵鷺主要吃多種的魚類、軟體動物及兩棲類。
非洲琵鷺被列在《非洲-歐亞大陸遷徙水鳥保護協定》之下。
